name: Role Swap
description: Players exchange roles, seats, characters, or responsibilities under validated session rules.
design_notes: Role swaps are sensitive because they transfer permissions, UI, input authority, and sometimes camera ownership. The design should define who can request a swap, whether both players must confirm, what happens to active abilities, and how reconnect or rollback restores the committed role state.
design_purpose: Let teams adapt when players prefer different duties or a critical role becomes unavailable.
Support learning, accessibility, and cooperative problem solving without restarting the session.
Create moments where responsibilities shift in response to objectives, vehicles, or emergencies.
player_fantasy: Players feel they can hand off responsibility cleanly when the team needs a different arrangement.

parameters.name: role_swap_request_id
parameters.description: Request identifier used to confirm, cancel, audit, or reject a pending role exchange.

parameters.name: source_player_id
parameters.description: Player initiating or giving up a role in the swap transaction.

parameters.name: target_player_id
parameters.description: Player receiving or exchanging a role in the swap transaction.

parameters.name: source_role_id
parameters.description: Role held by the source player before the transaction commits.

parameters.name: target_role_id
parameters.description: Role held by the target player before the transaction commits.

parameters.name: swap_commit_tick
parameters.description: Authoritative tick where role permissions, UI, and input ownership change together.

edge_cases: A swap request is accepted while one player is dead, disconnected, stunned, or in a non-interruptible action.
Role UI changes before the server commits permissions and lets a player issue invalid commands.
A vehicle or station seat changes owner while physics, camera, or input prediction is mid-update.
A reconnecting player returns after their role was reassigned to keep the session playable.
common_bugs: Only client UI swaps roles while server-side permission checks still use the old role_id.
Active cooldowns, inventory locks, or objective ownership are copied twice or lost during transfer.
Two swap requests commit in the same tick and leave a player with no role or two roles.
Swap cancellation restores names and icons but not camera, input mapping, or hidden-information filters.
balancing_notes: Role swapping improves flexibility, but unrestricted swaps can bypass role weaknesses or encounter constraints.
Mutual confirmation prevents accidental griefing, while emergency takeover rules help when a player disconnects.
Lockouts are useful after swaps, but long lockouts can trap players in inaccessible responsibilities.
accessibility_notes: Allow role handoff for players who need a lower-intensity task during difficult sequences.
Show swap requests, consequences, and confirmation states in text so voice chat is not required.

implementation_notes.unity: Treat role swap as a server transaction that checks source_player_id, target_player_id, current role ids, lockouts, and active interactions before committing. After commit, replicate role_id, authority ownership, UI profile, and camera target in one versioned state update.
implementation_notes.godot: Use an authoritative session service for role_swap_request_id lifecycle. RPCs should request, accept, cancel, and commit swaps; client nodes rebuild input and UI from the committed role snapshot rather than locally exchanging labels.
implementation_notes.unreal: GameMode or a role subsystem should validate and commit swaps, updating PlayerState role data and any possessed pawns, seats, or ability components. Use server RPCs for requests and client RPCs only for presentation after commit.
implementation_notes.web: Keep pending swaps on the server with request ids and expected role versions. Browser clients can display confirmations, but the committed swap snapshot should be the only source of permission, UI, and hidden-information changes.

example_games.title: Lovers in a Dangerous Spacetime
example_games.note: Uses player movement between ship stations to shift responsibilities.

example_games.title: Overcooked
example_games.note: Often creates informal role handoffs as players swap tasks during a level.
